* [HISTORY & CULTURE](https://www.nationalgeographic.com/history)

# Inside Kowloon Walled City—a lawless metropolis where anarchy reigned in Hong Kong

Known as “the city of darkness,” Kowloon Walled City was a crowded, tangled metropolis of 60,000 people with little political or legal oversight.

The Walled City's interconnected rooftops were a combination of playground, laundry room, dumping ground, relaxation area, and observation deck for aircraft approaching nearby Kai Tak airport.

Photograph By Greg Girard

ByErin Blakemore

Published May 7, 2025

Until 1994, a tiny subsection of Hong Kong was dominated by a vertical city—a place so dense, it was hard to imagine how its 30,000 or more residents could survive there.

Known as Kowloon Walled City, the area was littered with trash, cluttered with crime, and renowned for its anarchy. Lawless and largely forgotten by both Chinese and Hong Kong officials, the city “has always aroused curiosity and fear, and few dared venture inside,” historian Elizabeth Sinn writes in the [*Journal of the Hong Kong Branch of the Royal Asiatic Society*](https://www.jstor.org/stable/23886841).

But how did Kowloon Walled City become so chaotic—and why did people choose to live there? Once called the “city of darkness,” this area was so tangled and contested that its legacy still lives on decades after its eventual destruction.

Kowloon, Hong Kong military encampments on land and fleets in the bay during the Second China War.

Photograph By Felice Beato, Wellcome Collection (Top) (Left) and Photograph By Felice Beato, Wellcome Collection (Bottom) (Right)

## The origins of Kowloon Walled City

Though it would later become known for its lawlessness, Kowloon Walled City was originally a military and governmental center. Established as an Imperial Chinese signal station in 1668, the eventual city within a city was located on the northeast side of the Kowloon Peninsula that partially makes up Hong Kong.

([*The history of Hong Kong, visualized.*](https://www.nationalgeographic.com/culture/article/hong-kong-history-visualized?loggedin=true&rnd=1745595703330))

In 1843, in the aftermath of the First Opium War, British colonial forces took over Hong Kong. But the walled city—so named because of fortifications like cannons, a gate, and watchtowers—remained in Chinese hands during the first days of the British occupation. The British colonial government gave the military garrison a special administrative status, and over the years its Chinese inhabitants added a shopping area, customs house, school, pier, and other features.

Although it was seen by many as a necessary shield against British influence in the region, the city was nonetheless open to non-Chinese people. British colonists, Chinese visitors, and others flocked to the 6.4-acre walled city, which soon became a hotspot for gambling and recreation.

In Kowloon, there were 300 interconnected rooftops where kids would often play.

Photograph By Greg Girard

## Contestation between Britain and China over the city

Then, in 1898, the British and Chinese [signed the Convention for the Extension of Hong Kong Territory](https://archive.org/details/jstor-2212073/page/n1/mode/2up), a 99-year lease that would continue British rule in Hong Kong for another century. Now considered one of a series of “unequal treaties” that reinforced British colonial rule, the treaty at first excluded Kowloon City from British occupation.

The agreement came with a condition: Chinese officials were only given jurisdiction in the area as long as they didn’t disrupt British colonial attempts to “defend” Hong Kong. Just one year later, in 1899, the British decided that the city’s governor was assisting resistance against British colonial rule. In response, the British overran Kowloon City, occupied it, and declared they would oversee it in the future.

A police officer doing a stop and search, likely looking for drugs. This was a normal practice at the time, when officers were often looking for people who illegally escaped the mainland into Hong Kong.

Photograph By Greg Girard

The view from the outskirts of the city.

Photograph By Ian Lambot, Blue Lotus Gallery

At this point, the walled city was contested territory, claimed by both the British and Chinese. Soon, writes archaeologist and architecture expert Y.L. [Lucy Wang](https://arthistory.columbia.edu/content/y-l-lucy-wang) in [*Architectural Histories*](https://journal.eahn.org/article/id/8292/), “its population had been depleted and its buildings emptied, a stark reality that corroborated its ambiguous status.”

The city’s questionable status was complicated even further by the unrest that accompanied [the fall of Qing China and the foundation of the Republic of China in 1912](https://www.taiwan.gov.tw/content_3.php). The new republic had a chaotic start as provinces once ruled by the Chinese imperial government were taken over by warlords, and civilians were threatened by violent conflicts.

([*How Hong Kong’s complex history explains its current crisis with China.*](https://www.nationalgeographic.com/culture/article/hong-kong-history-explain-relationship-china?rnd=1745595701618&loggedin=true))

[As civil war raged on the Chinese mainland between 1927 and 1949](https://www.nationalarchives.gov.uk/education/resources/the-chinese-civil-war/), refugees flooded Hong Kong and found their way to the walled city. In the years that followed, the city’s population ballooned from just a few hundred residents to thousands, then tens of thousands.

An aerial view of Kowloon Walled City shows how crowded these buildings were at the time.

Photograph By Jodi Cobb, Nat Geo Image Collection

## A lawless city—and a home

Impoverished and isolated, the city’s new residents lived in political and legal limbo. Neither the colonial government nor the Chinese government regulated the city, but the British tried, and Beijing officials resented the attempts.

Thus, writes architectural historian Eunice Mei Feng in [*Resistant City: Histories, Maps And The Architecture Of Development*](https://www.google.com/books/edition/Resistant_City_Histories_Maps_And_The_Ar/4mLcDwAAQBAJ?hl=en&gbpv=0), the city became “a criminal hideout…an aggregation of illegal structures susceptible to fire and health hazards.” Ad-hoc buildings turned into multistory complexes connected by balconies and steep staircases. Gambling parlors, strip clubs, restaurants, and unregulated markets proliferated among the coffin-like buildings, and the city was riddled with theft and poverty.

During the Second World War, Japanese forces occupied Hong Kong and [tore down](https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Making_of_Hong_Kong/mH6rAgAAQBAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=kai%20tak%20airport%20kowloon%20walled%20city%201940&pg=PA27&printsec=frontcover) the city’s walls to extend a runway at a nearby airport. But even without a wall, the city was a place unto itself. “The disputed area is neither walled nor is it a city,” [*Paterson News*](https://www.newspapers.com/article/the-news/170933697/) wrote in 1960. “In reality it is a tiny enclave of sin and filth.”

Rooftops were a place of reprieve where children played and did homework. But the city below was dark and congested.

Photograph By Greg Girard

Planes from Kai Tak airport would often fly closely above the city while residents did their laundry on the rooftops.

Photograph By Greg Girard

Here, a man makes dough in a room covered in flour. Most of the city's shops and markets went unregulated.

Photograph By Jodi Cobb, Nat Geo Image collection

Though Hong Kong had laws, many non-criminal offenses weren't enforced due to the political status of the city.

Photograph By Greg Girard

Kowloon had limited access to city infrastructure such as garbage collection and running water. But for its residents—who moved there due to necessity, bankruptcy, or blight—it was home. In an [analysis](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.landusepol.2018.04.047) of resident oral histories, a group of Hong Kong-based scholars concluded that Kowloon Walled City residents had strong community ties, affordable housing, and a higher quality of life than international reportage usually depicted.

Topsy-turvy streets, ad-hoc residences, and crowded, tangled towers of homes and businesses gave the area a feel no other city could replicate.

“We walked along ‘streets’ no wider than my outstretched arms,” said U.S. foreign service officer Donald Michael Bishop in an [oral history](https://adst.org/OH%20TOCs/Bishop-Donald-Michael1.pdf). “Looking up, the small slice of sky between the buildings was obscured by a thicket of illegal electric wires tapping the current. I could make out one lonely star.... Had we turned into any alley, or climbed the stairs, we would have seen the dark and ugly sides of the Kowloon Walled City.”

But tensions festered over the legal status of the city and its residents. Hong Kong’s colonial forces regularly kicked out people they characterized as “squatters” and destroyed homes and buildings. Yet officials largely abandoned the city’s residents, leaving them on their own to build what a group of U.K. sociologists later [called](https://doi.org/10.1177/1741659017703681) “a form of makeshift community life, making do with what was available.”

It is estimated that before the city's demolition in 1994, as many as 60,000 people may have lived in Kowloon.

Photograph By Ian Lambot Ian Lambot, Blue Lotus Gallery

## The downfall of Kowloon Walled City

It is estimated that by the 1980s, up to 60,000 people may have lived in city boundaries. But time was running out for both the British colonial government and Kowloon Walled City. As China and the U.K. made plans for the eventual handover of Hong Kong to the Chinese, they announced in 1987 that the city would be demolished. The city was leveled in 1994 after nearly a decade spent resettling its residents, and is now a [park and archaeological site](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/ca/interactive-map/kowloon-walled-city-park.html).

Though known to the outside world as a den of vice and danger, Kowloon Walled City is still seen as an example of creativity and resilience in the face of neglect and repression. The “city of darkness” may be just a memory today, but it continues to capture curiosity, hinting at what can be accomplished by communities struggling for survival.

# Kowloon City

Explore Kowloon City, Hong Kong's multicultural hotspot, where vibrant communities, historic sites and the Kai Tak Sports Park offer a unique blend of culture, food and rich heritage.

 Articles needing cleanup from June 2019 [...] ## History

[edit]



  was leased by the British as part of the New Territories under the 1898 Second Convention of Peking for 99 years. Within New Kowloon is Kowloon City, an area of Hong Kong where the Kowloon Walled City used to be located. The Kowloon Walled City itself was demolished in 1993. The same area was called Kwun Fu Cheung (官富場) during the Song dynasty (960–1279). "New Kowloon" has remained part of the New Territories.

Statutorily, "Kowloon" is only the area south of Boundary Street and Stonecutters Island, but in common use, New Kowloon is not regarded as part of the New Territories, but as an integral part of the Kowloon urban area whether north or south of Boundary Street. [...] Kowloon (/ˌkaʊˈluːn/), formerly Kaulung, is one of the three areas of Hong Kong, along with Hong Kong Island and the New Territories. It is an urban area comprising the Kowloon Peninsula and New Kowloon. It has a population of 2,019,533 and a population density of 43,033/km2 (111,450/sq mi) in 2006. It is the smallest, second most populous, and most densely populated of the divisions.

## Location

[edit]

Kowloon is located directly north of Hong Kong Island across Victoria Harbour. It is bordered by the Lei Yue Mun strait to the east, Mei Foo Sun Chuen, Butterfly Valley, and Stonecutter's Island to the west, a mountain range, including Tate's Cairn and Lion Rock to the north and Victoria Harbour to the south.

"Kowloon" is a transliteration of 九龍. For other transliterations, see [九龍 (disambiguation)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/九龍_(disambiguation) "九龍 (disambiguation)").

**Kowloon** ([/ˌkaʊˈluːn/](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")), [formerly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Government_Cantonese_Romanization "Hong Kong Government Cantonese Romanization") **Kaulung**, is one of the [three areas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Areas_of_Hong_Kong "Areas of Hong Kong") of [Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), along with [Hong Kong Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Island "Hong Kong Island") and the [New Territories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Territories "New Territories"). It is an urban area comprising the [Kowloon Peninsula](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_Peninsula "Kowloon Peninsula") and [New Kowloon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Kowloon "New Kowloon"). It has a population of 2,019,533 and a population density of 43,033/km2 (111,450/sq mi) in 2006. It is the smallest, second most populous, and most densely populated of the divisions.

## Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kowloon&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Location")]

Kowloon is located directly north of [Hong Kong Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Island "Hong Kong Island") across [Victoria Harbour](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Victoria_Harbour "Victoria Harbour"). It is bordered by the [Lei Yue Mun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lei_Yue_Mun "Lei Yue Mun") strait to the east, [Mei Foo Sun Chuen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mei_Foo_Sun_Chuen "Mei Foo Sun Chuen"), [Butterfly Valley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Butterfly_Valley "Butterfly Valley"), and [Stonecutter's Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stonecutters_Island "Stonecutters Island") to the west, a mountain range, including [Tate's Cairn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tate's_Cairn "Tate's Cairn") and [Lion Rock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lion_Rock "Lion Rock") to the north and [Victoria Harbour](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Victoria_Harbour "Victoria Harbour") to the south.

## Administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kowloon&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Administration")]

Main articles: [Kowloon Central](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_Central_(disambiguation) "Kowloon Central (disambiguation)"), [Kowloon East](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_East "Kowloon East"), and [Kowloon West](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_West "Kowloon West")

Kowloon comprises the following [districts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Districts_of_Hong_Kong "Districts of Hong Kong"):

* [Kowloon City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_City_District "Kowloon City District")
* [Kwun Tong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kwun_Tong_District "Kwun Tong District")
* [Sham Shui Po](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sham_Shui_Po_District "Sham Shui Po District")
* [Wong Tai Sin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wong_Tai_Sin_District "Wong Tai Sin District")
* [Yau Tsim Mong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yau_Tsim_Mong_District "Yau Tsim Mong District")

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kowloon&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Name")]

The name **Kowloon** ([Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Traditional_Chinese "Traditional Chinese"): 九龍 lit. "Nine Dragons") alludes to eight mountains and a Chinese emperor: [Kowloon Peak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_Peak "Kowloon Peak"), [Tung Shan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tung_Shan_(mountain) "Tung Shan (mountain)"), [Tate's Cairn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tate's_Cairn "Tate's Cairn"), [Temple Hill](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Temple_Hill_(Hong_Kong) "Temple Hill (Hong Kong)"), [Unicorn Ridge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unicorn_Ridge "Unicorn Ridge"), [Lion Rock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lion_Rock "Lion Rock"), [Beacon Hill](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beacon_Hill_(Hong_Kong) "Beacon Hill (Hong Kong)"), [Crow's Nest](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crow's_Nest_(Hong_Kong) "Crow's Nest (Hong Kong)") and [Emperor Bing of Song](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emperor_Bing_of_Song "Emperor Bing of Song").[[1]](#cite_note-Fallon-1) It has been variously transcribed and translated over the years as **Kaulung** or **Kawloong**, among others .[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kowloon&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Kowloon_City%2C_Mainland%2C_opposite_Hong_Kong.jpg/250px-Kowloon_City%2C_Mainland%2C_opposite_Hong_Kong.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Kowloon_City,_Mainland,_opposite_Hong_Kong.jpg)

Kowloon c. 1868, depicting the [Qing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qing_empire "Qing empire")-era [Kowloon Walled City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_Walled_City "Kowloon Walled City") and [Lion Rock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lion_Rock "Lion Rock") (in the background)

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Victoria_City_and_Kowloon_1915.jpg/250px-Victoria_City_and_Kowloon_1915.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Victoria_City_and_Kowloon_1915.jpg)

Map of Kowloon in 1915

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Hong_Kong_Kai_Tak_Airport_1971.jpg/250px-Hong_Kong_Kai_Tak_Airport_1971.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_Kai_Tak_Airport_1971.jpg)

Hong Kong's old airport, [Kai Tak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kai_Tak_Airport "Kai Tak Airport"), was located in Kowloon Bay.

The part of Kowloon south of [Boundary Street](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boundary_Street "Boundary Street"), together with [Stonecutters Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stonecutters_Island "Stonecutters Island"), was ceded by [Qing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qing_empire "Qing empire") China to the United Kingdom under the [Convention of Peking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convention_of_Peking "Convention of Peking") of 1860. For many years the area remained largely undeveloped and was used by the British mainly for tiger-hunting expeditions.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[*[self-published source?](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] The part of Kowloon north of Boundary Street ([New Kowloon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Kowloon "New Kowloon")) was leased by the British as part of the [New Territories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Territories "New Territories") under the 1898 [Second Convention of Peking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convention_between_the_United_Kingdom_and_China,_Respecting_an_Extension_of_Hong_Kong_Territory "Convention between the United Kingdom and China, Respecting an Extension of Hong Kong Territory") for 99 years. Within New Kowloon is Kowloon City, an [area of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_areas_of_Hong_Kong "List of areas of Hong Kong") where the [Kowloon Walled City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_Walled_City "Kowloon Walled City") used to be located. The Kowloon Walled City itself was demolished in 1993. The same area was called Kwun Fu Cheung (官富場) during the [Song dynasty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") (960–1279). "New Kowloon" has remained part of the [New Territories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Territories "New Territories").

Statutorily, "Kowloon" is only the area south of Boundary Street and Stonecutters Island, but in common use, New Kowloon is not regarded as part of the New Territories, but as an integral part of the Kowloon urban area whether north or south of Boundary Street.

Large-scale development of Kowloon began in the early 20th century, with the construction of the [Kowloon-Canton Railway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon-Canton_Railway_Corporation "Kowloon-Canton Railway Corporation") and the [Kowloon Wharf](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_Wharf "Kowloon Wharf"), but because of the close proximity of Kowloon's built-up area to [Kai Tak Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kai_Tak_Airport "Kai Tak Airport"), building construction was limited by flight paths. As a result, compared to Hong Kong Island, Kowloon had a much lower skyline.[[1]](#cite_note-Fallon-1) After [World War II](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Kowloon became extremely congested when [slums](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Slum "Slum") for [refugees](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Refugee "Refugee") from the newly-established People's Republic of China gave way to [public housing estates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_housing_in_Hong_Kong "Public housing in Hong Kong"), mixed with private residential, commercial, and industrial areas.

The area of [reclaimed land](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Land_reclamation_in_Hong_Kong "Land reclamation in Hong Kong") that is now known as [West Kowloon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Kowloon "West Kowloon") was once home to a [dockyard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dockyard "Dockyard") for the [Royal Navy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy").

The 1911 census recorded a population of 7,306, with most being [Hakka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hakka "Hakka").[[4]](#cite_note-4) The [invasion of China by Japan in 1937](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War") caused the population of Kowloon to grow drastically. Between 1937 and 1939, 750,000 refugees arrived in Kowloon and nearby areas, with many not having residence.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kowloon&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Demographics")]

As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Kowloon&action=edit), 2,108,419 people lived in Kowloon.[[6]](#cite_note-hkcensus-6)

About 94.2% of Kowloon's residents are of [Han Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Han_Chinese "Han Chinese")[*[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] ethnicity. The largest ethnic minority groups are [Indonesians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indonesians_in_Hong_Kong "Indonesians in Hong Kong") (1.8%), [Filipinos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Filipinos_in_Hong_Kong "Filipinos in Hong Kong") (1.5%), [Indians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indians_in_Hong_Kong "Indians in Hong Kong") (0.5%), [Nepalese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nepalese_in_Hong_Kong "Nepalese in Hong Kong") (0.4%), and [British](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_people "British people") (0.3%).[[6]](#cite_note-hkcensus-6) Around 86% of Kowloon's residents use [Cantonese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantonese_language "Cantonese language") as their usual language, while 2.3% use English and 1.2% use [Mandarin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese").[[6]](#cite_note-hkcensus-6)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kowloon&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Western_Harbour_Tunnel.JPG/250px-Western_Harbour_Tunnel.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Western_Harbour_Tunnel.JPG)

[Western Harbour Crossing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Western_Harbour_Crossing "Western Harbour Crossing"), one of the tunnels that link Kowloon with Hong Kong Island

Kowloon is connected to Hong Kong Island by two road-only [tunnels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tunnel "Tunnel") (the [Cross-Harbour Tunnel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross-Harbour_Tunnel "Cross-Harbour Tunnel") and the [Western Harbour Crossing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Western_Harbour_Crossing "Western Harbour Crossing")), three MTR railway tunnels ([Tsuen Wan line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tsuen_Wan_line "Tsuen Wan line"), [Tung Chung line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tung_Chung_line "Tung Chung line")/[Airport Express](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Airport_Express_(MTR) "Airport Express (MTR)") and [East Rail](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shatin_to_Central_Link#Cross_Harbour_Section "Shatin to Central Link")) and one combined road and MTR rail link tunnel ([Eastern Harbour Tunnel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eastern_Harbour_Tunnel "Eastern Harbour Tunnel"), containing the [Tseung Kwan O line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tseung_Kwan_O_line "Tseung Kwan O line") and road traffic in separate parallel conduits). No bridges connect the island and Kowloon.
